questions.question: What is Promptable Concept Segmentation (PCS), introduced by SAM 3?
options: Segmenting all instances of a concept described by a short noun phrase or image exemplar in a single forward pass, returning masks plus unique instance IDs
A visual-prompt (point/box) segmentation like classic SAM — one prompt per instance
A multi-step cascade: a text detector produces boxes, then SAM segments each box
A tracking-only mode that propagates a mask through video frames
questions.explanation: PCS is SAM 3's signature capability. Pass 'yellow school bus' or an image exemplar; SAM 3 returns every matching instance with its own mask and unique ID, plus a presence score indicating whether the concept exists at all. Classic SAM needed one click or box prompt per instance. PCS collapses the entire detect-then-segment cascade into one forward pass.

questions.question: SAM 3 uses a shared ViT backbone read by both the detector head and the memory-based tracker. How does this differ from the Grounded SAM 2 cascade, and what advantage does it provide?
options: A shared backbone skips self-attention, making it faster for high-resolution images

The shared backbone gives detector and tracker access to the same feature map, eliminating redundant image encoding and error accumulation; the Grounded SAM 2 cascade runs Grounding DINO and SAM 2 as two separate forward passes with independent encoders
A shared backbone always produces larger masks than a two-model cascade
A shared ViT backbone requires half the GPU memory of any cascade approach
questions.explanation: Grounded SAM 2 runs a separate open-vocabulary detector (e.g. Grounding DINO) followed by SAM 2 — two full image encodings with independent feature extractors, meaning errors from the detector compound into SAM 2. SAM 3's shared backbone computes image features once, then both the detector head and the memory-based tracker read from the same representation. This removes redundant computation and enables end-to-end training on the full detect-segment objective.

questions.question: The `split_concepts(sentence)` helper processes the input 'cats, dogs and balloons'. What does it return, and why is this necessary for SAM 3?
options: A dictionary mapping each concept to its expected bounding box region
The sentence unchanged, since SAM 3 handles multi-concept strings natively
['cats', 'dogs', 'balloons'] — individual noun phrases split on comma and 'and', because SAM 3 accepts one concept prompt per forward pass and multi-concept queries must be looped or batched
An image crop for each noun phrase extracted by the vision encoder
questions.explanation: `split_concepts` heuristically replaces 'and ' with ',' then splits on comma/semicolon separators, stripping whitespace. For 'cats, dogs and balloons' it produces ['cats', 'dogs', 'balloons']. This is necessary because SAM 3's API accepts one concept per call — a single prompt produces all matching instances of that concept. To segment multiple concepts in one image, callers must loop over the list and merge results.

questions.question: SAM 3 was trained on 4 million unique concepts using an iterative AI+human data engine, and evaluated on the SA-CO benchmark. What makes SA-CO's 270K concepts significant?
options: SA-CO is the first benchmark to include tracking alongside segmentation
SA-CO was created specifically to expose Grounded SAM 2's failure modes
SA-CO uses video-only inputs, ruling out static-image models

270K concepts is 50× larger than prior open-vocabulary benchmarks, testing genuine generalisation beyond common-concept memorisation; SAM 3 reaches 75-80% of human performance on SA-CO and doubles existing systems
questions.explanation: Prior open-vocabulary benchmarks covered a few thousand concepts, making it easy for models trained on large web data to appear to generalise when they are actually memorising common categories. SA-CO's 270K unique concepts stress-test true compositional and rare-concept generalisation. SAM 3's 75-80% human performance and 2× improvement over prior systems on this harder benchmark is what substantiates its open-vocabulary claims.

questions.question: SAM 3's presence head produces what output, and why is it architecturally separate from the localisation head?
options: A ranked list of candidate bounding boxes, reranked by concept similarity
A shared memory bank that replaces per-instance tracking state

A scalar probability that the queried concept exists in the image at all, decoupled from localisation; this lets the model return zero detections cleanly and reduces false positives on absent concepts
The final binary mask, output directly without further decoding
questions.explanation: The presence head separates the 'is this here?' decision from the 'where is it?' decision. A model that must produce boxes whenever prompted tends to hallucinate matches for absent concepts. With a presence head, SAM 3 can return zero detections cleanly. This also improves discrimination between closely related prompts (e.g. 'a player in white' vs 'a player in red') and is one of the architectural innovations that distinguish SAM 3 from the Grounded SAM 2 cascade.

questions.question: SAM 3.1 Object Multiplex (March 2026) introduced a shared-memory mechanism for video tracking. What does it replace, and what is the practical benefit?
options: It replaces the ViT backbone with a lighter CNN for edge deployment
Per-instance separate memory banks; Multiplex collapses them into one shared memory with per-instance queries so tracking N objects costs substantially less memory and compute while keeping accuracy
The presence head, which was found to cause false negatives in crowded scenes
The decoupled detector — Multiplex merges detection and tracking into a single head
questions.explanation: Prior SAM 2 / SAM 3 tracking maintained one memory bank per tracked instance, so memory and compute grew linearly with the number of tracked objects. Object Multiplex introduces a single shared memory with per-instance queries that fetch instance-specific features from that shared store. Many-instance tracking (crowds, dense multi-object scenes) is now efficient — essential for real annotation and robotics workloads that track dozens of objects simultaneously.
